content.problemStatement:
3 logicians walk into a bar. The bartender asks them, "Does everyone want a beer?".

Logician 1 replies "I don't know".

Then, logician 2 replies "I don't know".

Finally, logician 3 replies "Yes".

  Now, coming to the actual problem.

NNN logicians numbered 1,2,…,N1, 2, \ldots, N1,2,…,N walk into a bar.

You are given a binary string SSS where Si=1S_i = 1Si​=1 if and only if the iii-th logician wants beer for himself, otherwise Si=0S_i = 0Si​=0.

Note that the logicians don't know the string SSS — each one only knows whether they themself want a beer.

They are also perfect logicians, and hence can extract information from hearing what the previous logicians reply.

The bartender asks them, "Does everyone want a beer?".

Predict what will be the replies of logician 1,2,…,N1, 2, \ldots, N1,2,…,N if they reply in that order.

Once again, note that logician iii only has the following information:


SiS_iSi​, i.e, whether he himself wants a beer or not; and
The replies of logicians 1,2,3,…,i−11, 2, 3, \ldots, i-11,2,3,…,i−1.
Output "IDK" for "I Don't know", "YES" or "NO" with their respective meanings.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case one integer NNN, the number of logicians.
The second line contains a binary string SSS of length NNN, representing whether each logician wants beer or not.




Output Format
For each test case, output NNN lines: the responses of logicians 1,2,…,N1, 2, \ldots, N1,2,…,N in order.


Each character of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e, the strings `no`, `No`, `nO`, and `NO` will all be treated as equivalent.
constraints: S consists only of characters '0' and '1'
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 100). The second line contains a binary string S of length N, where S_i = '1' means the i‑th logician wants a beer and '0' means he does not.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output N lines. The i‑th line is the answer of logician i: "YES" if he can be certain that everyone wants a beer, "NO" if he can be certain that not everyone wants a beer, otherwise "IDK" (I don't know). Answers of consecutive test cases are printed one after another without extra blank lines.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1 (111): all want beer, so first two cannot be sure (IDK) and the last knows the answer is YES.\nTest case 2 (00): the first logician already knows the answer is NO, and everyone after hearing a NO also answers NO.

solution.approach:
Let firstZero be the smallest index (1‑based) such that S[firstZero] = '0'. If no such index exists, every logician wants a beer. In that case the first N‑1 logicians cannot be sure (they answer IDK) and the last one knows the answer is YES. If a zero exists, any logician before firstZero sees only '1' for himself and has heard only IDK answers, so he cannot be sure and answers IDK. The logician at position firstZero knows his own desire is '0' and therefore answers NO. All later logicians have already heard a NO, which tells them that not everyone wants a beer, so they also answer NO. The algorithm is therefore:
1. Scan S from left to right to find firstZero (or conclude that it does not exist).
2. For i = 1 … N:
   - if firstZero does not exist: output "IDK" for i < N, otherwise "YES".
   - else if i < firstZero: output "IDK".
   - else output "NO".
The scan is O(N) per test case, and the output generation is also O(N).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(T·N) overall.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides the input string.
